content:
Sundari was a domed city, filled with impressive angular buildings, parks, and factories.  It was the governmental center of Mandalore, and the site of the SurveyCorps training facility on this planet. 

She’d been in Sundari, on Mandalore, a little over a month. She’d expected to enjoy it a lot more.  True, the planet had a very long history, and the near space in this system was crammed with wreckage and historic ruins, a tribute to the long history of strife and warfare in and around Mandalore.

That was supposed to be in the past, of course.  Mandalore was part of the Republic and officially welcomed non-Mandalorians to their planet.  But Iyola felt differently.   She normally made friends easily, but she’d found the native Mandalorians to be polite at best, keeping her and the other SurveyCorps inductees at a distance.  As part of her language routine, she  watched local holodramas and holonet news, and detected a tone of - was it disdain?  - for the Republic.  She was an interloper.  What was that word?  *Aruetii.*  An outsider. It was one step away from the word for enemy.  

But she’d finally made a Mandalorian friend, Saviin Deshra, one of the few Mandalorians in the SurveyCorps**.  “Everyone here remembers the Dral’Han,”** Saviin had told her when Iyola had confessed that she found Mandalorians unfriendly. ** “Mandalorians have long memories and short tempers. Don’t take it personally.”  **

Saviin spoke about the Dral-Han like something that had happened within her lifetime, but when Iyola researched it, she discovered that the Dral’Han was what she knew of as the Excision, a devastating attack by the Republic on Mandalore over 500 years ago. The Excision was responsible for blasting half the planet into white-hot desert wastes, and that was why Sundari was in a dome, protecting the city from its own world.

It was thanks to the Excision and the long history of Mandalorian warfare that the Mandalorian system offered such a rich trove of opportunities for wreckage survey training.  A lot of the coursework in the SurveyCorps was dull, but the hands-on training was thrilling.  

She’d just come back the day before.  Her instructor had directed Iyola’s group to document and record any wreckage and artifacts found, but not to take anything, and not to disturb any bodies. The Mandalorians were especially sensitive about a metal found on their planet, beskar.

And she’d complied - well mostly. When investigating the shell of a old Davaab-class starfigher, she’d found, detached from a loose pauldron, a really awesome orange-enameled emblem of some kind of leonid.  

She’d reasoned that the wreck might disintegrate or be destroyed at any time.  She honestly probably shouldn’t have boarded it in the first place, if she was following protocol.  She left the pauldron but took the leonid signet. She had tucked it away in her EVA suit, and no one was the wiser. 

 Of course, she had to fill out a FHI - found historic item - report for the rest of the evening, just to mention that one piece of old beskar. Imagine if she’d reported her new leonid symbol.

**“You want to go to Keldabe?”** Saviin said doubtfully, looking up from her datapad.  Her golden-blonde hair was in the weird angular bob that was popular in Sundari right now.  Iyola was keeping her braided ponytail.  

**“Oh yeah,” **Iyola said.  **“I’ve heard that there is a great street market in the old city today .  Real traditional architecture, more authentic.  And better cantinas.” **

**“The market is fun,” ** allowed Saviin. ** “But I’m not sure it’s safe for, well, for non-Mandalorians.”**

**“Why not?”** asked Iyola in genuine confusion. **“It’s the capital of Mandalore!”**

Saviin hesitated.  **“It is more traditional.  And especially on market days, some of the moon people come to Keldabe.  They call Mandalorians like me faithless because we don’t live our lives encased in beskar, because we have a high council instead of recognizing whichever of their warriors is currently on top. They usually don’t cause trouble, but they don’t appreciate people like me, and especially like you.”**  

Nothing Saviin had said made Iyola want to avoid Keldabe.  *Quite the contrary. * **“Oh come on, Saviin. **|| Don’t be a, a dikut. Make clan Deshra proud.” ||

 Saviin looked at Iyola with a small smile on her lips. **“Your Mando’a is improving.  All right, we’ll go.  I’ll take you. But Iyola, don’t ever call me a dikut again.” **

Outside the climate-controlled dome, the weather was cool and windy.  Iyola loved it.  It reminded her of Rhinnal. There were trees here, and the air smelled like resin and salt. It was poncho weather.  She and Saviin both wore one - hers was green, Saviin’s white - but she kept her hood down.  She wanted to see everything.

Here in Keldabe, streets were narrow and rambling, not the wide avenue grid of Sundari.  The buildings were a hodgepodge of styles and materials - durasteel, stone, even wood.  Some of them leaned over the streets into arches. In the near distance, there were modern buildings, like the sky-high tower that Saviin told her was the headquarters of MandalMotors.  

Everywhere she looked, people were crowding the streets and storefronts and stalls - laughing, bargaining, drinking shig.  There were more non-humans here than in Sundari.  A Togruta vendor waved her over, his dark blue eyes twinkling. 

**“Su’cuy, ad’ika!” ** he greeted them, before taking another look at Iyola and switching to Basic.  **“Take a look at my wares. Fine jewelry,  and traditional Mandalorian handicrafts.”** He showed her a statuette, carved from banded stone, of a very ugly tusked beast.   

**“A Mythosaur,” **said Iyola.

**“Yes, good!”  **said the Togruta.  **“The honored symbol of my people.”  **

 Saviin glanced at the item.  **“Tourist stuff.”  **The vendor stiffened, but Saviin ignored him.** “Are you going to be all right by yourself for an hour?  I have to go to my appointment, but let’s meet up in front of the Oyu’baat - that’s the huge cantina with a hotel, you can’t miss it.  Just stay on this main street in the meantime.” **

**“I’m great,”** Iyola said. **“I’ll comm you if I’m running late.”  **

**“Be good,”** said Saviin. She drew her fur-trimmed hood around her face and set off towards a tall modern-looking tower.

Feeling like she needed to make up for Saviin's insult, Iyola looked over the Togruta vendor’s selection.  It occurred to her that she could use some kind of string or chain to put the leonid emblem on.  She sighted a thick hide cord that looked durable, attached to an azurite pendant of mediocre quality.  

**“How much is that necklace?”** she asked.  

After some spirited bargaining she knocked the price down by a third, though she was pretty sure she was still overpaying.  The stone pendant was junk, but the cord was a durable and utilitarian item, made as it was of shatual hide. 

There was a trace of frost in the air.  Iyola happily browsed the stores and stalls, stopping for a steaming mug of shig spiked with some kind of booze. She saw a passageway leading off the main street, and impulsively went down it.  As she wandered a maze of side streets, she saw a male human wearing what looked like a full suit of battle armor, painted golden yellow and tan.  

He had a purple half-cape draped over his shoulder, with an emblem  that looked like a pair of wings. He was carrying a helmet.  He was young, with a long face and close-cropped red hair.  He caught Iyola’s curious gaze.”

**“Hi!”**  Iyola said.

He said nothing, staring back at her until she’d passed him.  This must be one of the moon people Saviin had mentioned, the Concordians. The only Mandalorians she’d seen wearing armor in Sundari were the governmental guards.

She kept exploring.  The streets here seemed older, some so narrow she could practically span them with her arms.  She entered an alleyway that had tiny storefronts crammed full of a different mix of goods than Iyola had seen on the main street.  Instead of jewelry and pog wraps,  this narrow strip, paved with ancient, chipped tiles, had knives and  half-cloaks and obsolete electronics and dusty ancient-looking objects.  Iyola’s interest was immediately piqued.  

She smiled in greeting at the store vendors, but they looked through her like she wasn’t there.  She sifted through a pile of kama and cloaks.  The weave was tough, shot through with durasteel threading. These were meant for armored Mandalorians, not Sundari fashionistas.  

She moved to another stall, where the proprietor, a solidly built woman wearing an asymmetrical red cape with matching gloves, studiously ignored her.  There was an appealing array of gloves for sale here -  they looked durable but flexible, and came in a variety of materials and colors.

**“Are you lost?” ** a man asked her in Basic.

Iyola didn’t look up.  **“No, are you?”**   She could see him looming out of the corner of her eye, a gap-toothed man with a yellow-white beard and an expression she did not like at all.   She continued to examine the gloves on display, but sensed movement.

  He reached out to grab her wrist, but Iyola quickly leapt back out of reach, with loth-cat agility.

The vendor turned around. **“Garu!”**  she snapped.  **“Nar’sheb!”**  He looked at the red-cloaked woman, then slunk away. 

**“They are osi-yaim,” **she said. ** “How do you say it in Basic - “**

**“Loser,”**  replied Iyola.  **“Big loser.”  **

The woman found this hilarious.  She laughed so hard that her short black curly hair bounced around her head.  She  finally gathered herself, wiping the tears from her dark eyes. “**Yes, loser.”  **she said.  **“Always loses.”  **She looked at Iyola, seemingly for the first time.

Iyola continued to enjoy browsing the wares. The gloves were beautifully constructed,  made of bantha leather or shatual hide. Some were lined with sheared fur, and a few were constructed of some kind of tough scale-skin that Iyola couldn’t identify.  She finally decided on a pair of emerald green gloves with scale-skin insets on the palms.  They fit her perfectly.   

Iyola asked for the price, and the woman quoted her an amount that seemed fair, though pricey.  Something about the set of the woman’s jaw told her bargaining would not be welcome.

**“Sold,”** Iyola said.  Over the vendor’s shoulder, Iyola spotted a half-open crate of what looked like metal gauntlets.

**“How much are those?”** she asked, handing the seller the credits.

**“Those aren’t for you,”** said the vendor, firmly but not unkindly.  She gave Iyola the gloves.

**“Thank you,” **said Iyola.  She glanced at her chrono.  **“I should get back.  I need to meet my friend at the Oyu’baat.”**

**“My child Cebur is lazy, watching pod racing.”** said the woman.  **“They can guide you if you wish.” **

**“Thank you, but I can find my way back,”** said Iyola.  **“I have a good sense of direction.” **

**“I’m not lazy!”**  a boy called from behind a curtain. 

The woman paused for a second, studying Iyola.  **“Come.” ** she said.  She picked up a piece of black cord, and wrapped it around Iyola’s forehead twice, tying her loose hair back securely.  She motioned to the gloves, indicating that Iyola should put them on, which Iyola did.

****“Good,”* ***the store owner said. **“Now you look more, ah, nu’amyc.”  **

**“Thank you,”** said Iyola, flashing her brightest smile..

The woman gave her directions.  **“Go that way, and don’t smile at everyone.  You look stupid.” **

Iyola nodded, doing her best to keep her expression neutral.  The woman inclined her head slightly.

**“My name is Mav, of Awaud,” **the vendor said. ** “Be well, and don’t come back here again.” **

As Iyola approached what was clearly the Oyu’baat, her comm chimed.  Saviin was running a bit late, and Iyola told her that she’d meet inside the cantina.
 
This cantina looked like it had been here for ages.  The wood raining around the bar was worn smooth, and the walls were splashed with multiple hues of stains.  None of them looked fresh, fortunately.  There was a staircase and lift in the corner, likely to the hotel that shared the same space.

The customers were a mixed lot - Mandalorians of all sorts, flinty-eyed merchants, plenty of non and near-humans,and tourists - Iyola spotted a family of Aqualish clutching their  luggage who looked like they’d just stepped off the shuttle.  She wondered how they’d decided on Keldabe as a vacation site.  At a table directly across the bar, she noticed two armored Mandalorians, from  Concordia, most likely, including the red-haired man she’d seen on the streets earlier. She somehow knew he’d recognized her, though he didn’t look her way.

The tables in eyeshot had been taken, but she was able to find a few stools at the long, curved, scarred wooden bar, saving a seat for Saviin. 

The bartender, a tall human male with an impressive curly beard, looked at her.  Iyola did her best to suppress her instinct to smile, instead just lifting her chin in acknowledgment. 

**“What do you want?” ** asked the bartender.  It took Iyola a minute to realize he’d asked her in Mando’a.

**“Netra’gal,”** said Iyola curtly.  The bartender nodded, soon returning with a glass of ale, so dark it was nearly black, and handed it to her. 

**“K’oyacyi,”** he said.  *Cheers. * She took a sip - it was delicious, and less sour than expected.

Saviin bustled in, wrapped up in her poncho, and slid onto the stool next to Iyola. 

**“I never get used to how cold and smelly it is outside Sundari,” **she said.   

**“That’s most of the galaxy,”** said Iyola, switching to Basic. “**You want a drink?” **

**“Please.  That netra’gal looks delicious.”**  Saviin stopped and looked at Iyola more closely. ** “Did you take the Creed in the last hour or something?  What’s with the hairwrap and the gloves?”**

“**Just exploring Mandalorian culture,**” Iyola said breezily.  **‘Helps with language acquisition.” ** She raised her hand to order another drink.  The bartender side-eyed her but went to get the ale. 

**“Well, if you want to do it right, you need to wear the  clan symbol,**” Saviin joked.  She moved her poncho aside, showing the jagged broken-star emblem of clan Deshra, a silvered pin on her tunic.

It was good to spend some time just hanging out as friends away from the SurveyCorp grind.  Saviin introduced her to a spicy liqueur, ap’ga, that made her tongue tingle.  They had a few glasses of that.

** “Can you keep a secret?”**

Saviin looked almost offended.  **“Of course I can.  I’m a Mandalorian.” **

Iyola hadn’t realized that secret-keeping was part of Mandalorian culture, but she forged ahead.  

**“So you remember that debris tag training operation from a few weeks ago?” **

**“Yes,” **Saviin shuddered.  **“I hate EVA suits. They make me claustrophobic.” **
 
**“So.” **  Iyola popped the azurite pendant out of the necklace, and placed the orange enamel leonid symbol into its setting.  It fit perfectly.  **“Look what I found on one of the wrecks.**” She put the cord around her neck.  **“Isn’t it pretty?” ** The leonid symbol was surprisingly heavy, and despite its age, the orange enamel had not chipped. 

Saviin picked up the sigil on the necklace and looked at it more closely.   Iyola leaned closer so the cord wouldn’t choke her.** “Iyola, this Eldar crest is really old.  I think it’s beskar.” **

**“I figured,” **said Iyola. “**It came from a wrecked Davaab fighter, about 2000 years old. Those ships  look like three metal plates glued together.”**  She could feel the ap’ga fizzing through her bloodstream. It felt good.

The redheaded Mandalorian approached the bar from the other side to get a drink as Iyola turned to refill their glasses of ap’ga.  She could practically feel his eyes bore into her; then he turned and spoke to  his companion. 

**“I think that one likes you, Iyola,”  **Saviin said.  They snickered.  The bartender poured them another glass each.

Now the two Concordians were getting up from their table. The woman tossed some credits on the bar without looking.  They were making a beeline for Iyola and Saviin.

**“Saviin.”  **Saviin was staring into the mid-distance, looking pretty drunk.  **“Hey Saviin, I think  they’re coming over here. Do you know them?”**

**“Do you think all Mandalorians know each other or something?”**  Saviin said, her words slightly slurred. ** “Who’s the di’kut now?” **

Iyola slid off her stool.  Her head swam a bit, but she gathered herself.  The female Mandalorian approached her.  She was a human woman who moved smoothly and with intention.  She wore black and green armor and carried a helmet tucked into the crook of her elbow.

**“You,”** she spat in Mando’a. **“I am Rusaan Eldar, of clan Eldar.  Do you claim my aliit?”**

**“Uh, no?”  **

Rusaan scowled.   *That was the wrong thing to say, apparently. *Saviin started to say something, but the red-haired man gestured at her menacingly.

**“Hey, leave my friend alone, you weirdo,“** said Iyola.  

**“Are you a Mandalorian?”** Rusaan asked.

Iyola raised her chin defiantly.  She was starting to get annoyed at all these stubborn, rude Madalorians. ** “No.”** she said loudly. 

**“Then you are a ge’hutuun,**” Rusaan spat.  **“Give me the aliik and perhaps I will let you crawl away.”**

The room seemed to grow quiet.  Patrons backed away from them.  In a booth in the corner, the Aqualish family stared at her with their opaque black eyes.

Iyola fingered the leonid symbol.  The beskar felt cool and solid, even through the glove.

**“Come and get it,”** said Iyola. She picked up her last glass of ap’ga and took a sip. 

Rusaan tossed her helmet on the bar, then lunged toward Iyola.  Iyola dodged and threw the glass of ap’ga in her face; the spicy liquid splashing into the Mandalorian’s eyes and mouth.  Coughing, Rusaan threw a punch at Iyola’s face, but Iyola managed to knock her arm aside.

**“No blasters,” **said the bartender casually, as he leaned out of the way.

*This is a mistake,* thought Iyola.  She turned toward the door.  The other woman clutched at her wrist and pulled her closer. Iyola stomped on her foot and, when the Mandalorian’s grip loosened momentarily, sharply shoved her in the face. 

**“Good one,”** said the red-haired man in Basic. 

Rusaan barked out a single word that Iyola did not recognize but was likely extremely obscene.

Iyola   jumped back,  and managed to bump directly into Saviin.  As she opened her mouth to reflexively apologize, Rusaan sprang forward and knocked her onto the floor. Iyola landed awkwardly, and then Rusaan was on her. 

The last thing Iyola remembered before losing consciousness was the extremely unpleasant sensation of being repeatedly punched in the throat and head.

Iyola opened her eyes.  She was laying on a strange bed. Four big round black eyes were staring directly into hers.  She blinked. The four eyes blinked back. There were definitely four.  As her vision returned, she could see that the shiny black eyes were set in the fuzzy face of an Aqualish youngling.

**“Dada,” **the child announced, **“the human is awake.”**  

Iyola gingerly felt her head.  It was painful, but someone had bandaged it. The bacta wrappings didn’t help with the hangover, though.

 She felt like shit.  It was worse than when she’d fallen off the Hemmel glacier. 

**“I see that, Opi.  Thank you.” ** 

The two Aqualish adults were in the room, along with Saviin, who breathed a sigh of relief as Iyola sat up painfully, and handed Iyola a glass of water.  Iyola took it, but her hand was shaky and she ended up spilling half of it.

Iyola felt her throat, which was tender and swollen.

**“Yes, they took the crest from you,” ** said Saviin.  **“You’re incredibly lucky that that crazy Concordian didn’t kill you.”  **

Iyola looked at the Aqualish couple, wincing as she turned her head. **“Thank you for helping me.” **

The female Aqualish  nodded.  **“My husband Deno is a medic, and you definitely needed the help.” **

** “I’m sorry for causing you trouble.” **
 
The female Aqualish laughed.  **“Oh, don’t be.  This is the most exciting thing that’s happened to us on Mandalore so far.”  **
